beats.activity: Wake up, coffee, check tide tables and weather for the day ahead.
beats.reasoning: Sebastian rises early as a marine biologist—tide tables and weather are non-negotiable. Low energy from yesterday (40/100) means a quiet, routine morning at home before fieldwork.

beats.activity: Drive to Redmond lab; steady rain and SR-520 closure eastbound forces I-90 detour.
beats.reasoning: SR-520 eastbound closed 9am–noon; Sebastian must leave early and take I-90 to avoid the worst delays. Drizzle and rain make commute slower and more cautious.

beats.activity: Lab work: calibrate sensors, prep equipment for Puget Sound fieldwork, review water-quality data.
beats.reasoning: Routine lab prep before heading out on the water. Rain forecast until 2pm means indoor work is sensible; he'll be ready to deploy once conditions allow.

beats.activity: Quick bite at a food cart near the lab.
beats.reasoning: Sebastian knows every food truck rotation—he'll grab something quick and familiar rather than sit down, staying on schedule despite the rain.

beats.activity: Drive to Ballard; rain still steady, take surface streets to avoid SR-520 and bridge chaos.
beats.reasoning: Rain persists until 2pm; Sebastian heads west early to Puget Sound fieldwork site. Surface streets are slower but avoid the eastbound closure backlog entirely.

beats.activity: Fieldwork on Puget Sound: collect water samples, measure salinity and temperature, deploy sensors.
beats.reasoning: Rain clears by 2pm; Sebastian is ready to work the sound. This is where his passion lives—fieldwork in Puget Sound is his core mission, and he times it around the weather window.

beats.activity: Stop by Ballard Market for fresh sourdough starter and supplies.
beats.reasoning: Post-fieldwork habit: the baker knows him, and he refreshes his starter regularly. Quick errand on the way home, familiar ritual that anchors his week.

beats.activity: Drive home; take I-5 north and surface streets, traffic easing after midday closure window.
beats.reasoning: SR-520 closure ends at noon; I-5 is the most direct route home. He's tired after fieldwork and will decompress at home for the rest of the day.

beats.activity: Tend to sourdough starter, begin dough for tomorrow's bake, organize and dry field samples.
beats.reasoning: Post-fieldwork wind-down: sourdough baking is his meditative counterweight to the intensity of marine work. Fresh starter from the market goes straight to work.

beats.activity: Dinner: simple meal at home, review field notes and data from the day.
beats.reasoning: Quiet, solo dinner at home. Sebastian is routine-bound and prefers eating at home to processing the day's data and observations in his own space.

beats.activity: Develop film prints in the home darkroom; work on last month's Puget Sound photography.; Evening wind-down: tea, light reading, prepare for sleep.
beats.reasoning: Film photography is his creative outlet and mirrors his meditative sourdough practice. Red light and quiet development time suit his low energy and reflective mood.
Seven hours of sleep; Sebastian will wake early again tomorrow to check tide tables and plan the next fieldwork day.

diary: Gray morning, coffee in hand, checking tide tables like always. The rain started early and didn't let up—SR-520 was a mess, so I took the long way to Redmond, cursing the detour the whole drive. Lab work felt routine: sensors calibrated, data reviewed, the usual rhythm. Grabbed something quick at the food cart, then headed to Discovery Park with my equipment. There's something about being out on the Sound even in drizzle—the water doesn't care about the weather, and neither do I anymore. Collected good samples, got the sensors deployed. Stopped at Ballard Market on the way back for sourdough starter; the smell of fresh bread cut through the dampness. Home by mid-afternoon, hands busy with the dough, organizing samples. Spent the evening in the darkroom with my prints from last month—watching those images emerge in the red light, the Sound coming alive on paper. It's where I feel most myself, honestly.
thought_process: The bridge closure threw off my morning, but I've navigated Seattle's traffic chaos long enough not to let it rattle me. The rain actually felt right for fieldwork—less sun glare on the water, steadier conditions for sensor deployment. I chose the surface streets on the way back because I-90 would've been packed with all the eastbound spillover. The sourdough starter was an impulse; I needed something tactile and slow after a day of precision work. By evening, I was drawn to the darkroom naturally—that's where my mind goes when I'm processing the day. The photographs connect the science to something more personal, more real. Weather like this used to depress me, but now it just feels honest.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
